meta.title: About MC Test: Understanding the EDULEARN26 Paper
meta.target_audience: Participants in the EDULEARN26 session on student performance and predictive analytics

questions.question: 1. What is the primary purpose of MC Test?
options: To deliver high-stakes summative exams at institutional scale
To support formative MC practice with feedback and analytics
To replace instructor review in multiple-choice assessment workflows
To serve as a generic course-evaluation or opinion survey tool
questions.explanation: MC Test is built for formative multiple-choice practice, not to replace instructors. Its core value is linking practice items to feedback, learner-facing analytics, and pacing.

mini_glossary.Formative assessment: Assessment intended to support learning during the learning process.
mini_glossary.Multiple-choice practice: Practice format where learners choose one answer from predefined options.
mini_glossary.Learner-facing analytics: Analytics shown directly to learners to support reflection and study planning.

questions.question: 2. Which three cognitive levels does MC Test use for item weights?
options: Scoring, timing, and ranking
Memorization, collaboration, and presentation
Remembering, evaluating, and creating
Reproduction, application, and analysis
questions.explanation: MC Test uses a restricted three-level taxonomy inspired by Bloom: reproduction, application, and analysis.

mini_glossary.Reproduction: Recall of facts, definitions, or simple procedures.
mini_glossary.Application: Use of known concepts in a slightly varied context.
mini_glossary.Analysis: Interpretation, comparison, or inference based on given information.

questions.question: 3. Which framework is used to build the MC Test web application?
options: Static HTML pages without interactive backend logic
Moodle as the sole application and hosting layer
Streamlit as the Python web application framework
A spreadsheet-based macro authoring system
questions.explanation: MC Test is implemented as a Streamlit web application. That choice supports rapid development of interactive Python-based learning tools.

mini_glossary.Streamlit: A Python framework for building interactive web applications.
mini_glossary.Web application: A software system accessed through a browser.
mini_glossary.Backend logic: Server-side functionality that processes data and application behavior.

questions.question: 4. What three statuses does the Concept Mastery Columns view use?
options: Fast, medium, and slow response pacing bands
Public, private, and anonymous access classifications
Easy, medium, and hard item difficulty tiers
Understood, not attempted, and not understood
questions.explanation: Concept Mastery Columns group fine-grained concepts by learner performance. The three statuses are understood, not attempted, and not understood.

mini_glossary.Concept: A fine-grained learning target within a broader topic.
mini_glossary.Mastery: A heuristic indication that performance on a concept is sufficient.
mini_glossary.Not attempted: A status indicating that no answer evidence is available for a concept.

questions.question: 5. Which deployment goal best motivates an on-premises local LLM backend in MC Test?
options: Removing the need for instructor review
Guaranteeing perfect item quality
Reducing institutional data transfer risk
Hiding learner analytics from students
Eliminating the need for validation
questions.explanation: A local backend supports privacy-sensitive deployment by keeping prompts and learner data inside institutional infrastructure. It does not remove validation or instructor review.

mini_glossary.On-premises: Deployment within an institution's own infrastructure.
mini_glossary.Local LLM backend: A language-model service running locally rather than through an external API.
mini_glossary.Data-transfer risk: Risk that sensitive data leaves the institutional environment.

questions.question: 6. A learner scores well on reproduction items but poorly on application and analysis. Which MC Test view best reveals this cognitive profile?
options: Topic Performance Chart
Post-test glossary
Cognitive Radar Chart
System audit log
questions.explanation: The Cognitive Radar Chart summarizes performance across reproduction, application, and analysis. It shows whether a learner mainly recalls facts or can also apply and analyze ideas.

mini_glossary.Cognitive Radar Chart: A visualization of performance across cognitive levels.
mini_glossary.Cognitive profile: A pattern of strengths and weaknesses across kinds of thinking.
mini_glossary.Application: Use of knowledge in a new but related context.

extended_explanation.title: Identifying the right dashboard view
extended_explanation.content: A radar chart is appropriate when the question concerns the balance between different cognitive demands. It helps distinguish recall-dominant performance from more balanced or reasoning-oriented performance.
steps: Identify that the problem concerns cognitive levels rather than topics.
Match reproduction, application, and analysis to the radar view.
Exclude topic-only and administrative views because they do not directly show this profile.

questions.question: 7. An instructor wants to generate multiple-choice items with an LLM without importing malformed output. Which MC Test design choice addresses this most directly?
options: Longer answer explanations attached to each answer option
A lower default threshold for concept mastery rules
Schema-driven JSON output with validation and repair
A larger catalog of unanswered response category labels
questions.explanation: Schema-driven JSON output makes generated items machine-checkable. Validation and repair loops catch structural problems before import and review.

mini_glossary.Schema: A formal structure that defines required fields and valid values.
mini_glossary.Validation: Checking whether generated output satisfies required rules.
mini_glossary.Repair loop: A follow-up correction process that fixes detected output errors.

extended_explanation.title: Why schema constraints matter
extended_explanation.content: LLM output should not be treated as valid by default. MC Test therefore treats generation as a controlled pipeline in which parseability, field completeness, and answer-index validity are checked explicitly.
steps: Recognize that malformed output is a structural problem.
Use a schema to define the required item format.
Apply validation to detect errors.
Use a repair loop for minimal correction before human review.

questions.question: 8. A generated item makes the correct option obvious because it is much longer than the distractors. Which MC Test mechanism is intended to reduce this cueing problem?
options: Time-Critical Override
Cohen's kappa calculation
Pseudonymous access
Option harmonization
Concept mastery thresholding
questions.explanation: Option harmonization revises answer choices so the keyed option is not obvious from length, syntax, or specificity. It preserves the answer key while improving item quality.

mini_glossary.Distractor: An incorrect but plausible answer option.
mini_glossary.Keyed option: The correct answer option in an MC item.
mini_glossary.Cueing: An unintended feature that helps learners guess the answer without knowing the content.

extended_explanation.title: Detecting option-level flaws
extended_explanation.content: Good MC items should not reward test-wise guessing. Harmonized options make it more likely that the item tests the intended concept rather than superficial textual cues.
steps: Notice that the correct option differs formally from the distractors.
Identify this as an item-writing flaw rather than a content problem alone.
Apply option harmonization to align length, syntax, and specificity.
Keep the semantic content and answer key stable.

questions.question: 9. A learner skips most analysis items in one topic but answers reproduction items in that topic correctly. Which dashboard view best separates this topic-level and cognitive-level pattern?
options: Concept Mastery Columns dashboard view
Topic × Cognitive Heatmap dashboard view
Single total score summary display
Item-level mini-glossary reference panel
questions.explanation: The Topic × Cognitive Heatmap crosses topics with cognitive levels. It helps show whether a difficulty is tied to one topic, one cognitive level, or their combination.

mini_glossary.Heatmap: A visualization that encodes values in a grid of cells.
mini_glossary.Topic: A broader curriculum unit or content area.
mini_glossary.Evidence volume: The amount of answer data available for interpretation.

extended_explanation.title: Interpreting a two-dimensional pattern
extended_explanation.content: A single score hides whether the learner has a content gap, a process-level gap, or an avoidance pattern. The heatmap makes these distinctions more visible.
steps: Identify that the problem involves both topic and cognitive level.
Select the view that combines topics as rows and cognitive levels as columns.
Use answered and total counts to judge whether the pattern has enough evidence.

questions.question: 10. Why does MC Test limit operational item labels to Bloom levels 1–3?
options: Because dashboards cannot display more than three colors
Because higher-order generation and validation are harder to keep reliable
Because learners should focus mainly on memorizing facts in STEM courses
Because formative assessment cannot use cognitive taxonomies
questions.explanation: MC Test focuses on Bloom-inspired levels 1–3 to improve verifiability and reduce semantic risk. Evaluation and creation tasks are harder to generate and validate reliably in closed multiple-choice formats.

mini_glossary.Bloom levels: A taxonomy for distinguishing levels of cognitive complexity.
mini_glossary.Evaluation: A higher-order process involving judgment against criteria.
mini_glossary.Creation: A higher-order process involving production of new artifacts or ideas.
mini_glossary.Verifiability: The degree to which correctness can be checked reliably.

extended_explanation.title: Rationale for restricting levels
extended_explanation.content: The restriction is a pragmatic design choice. It keeps the item model operational and lowers risk while still covering recall, transfer, and analytical reasoning.
steps: Recognize that MC Test targets closed multiple-choice items.
Link closed formats to constraints on what can be assessed reliably.
Prefer levels that can be reviewed and validated more consistently.
Retain human review because restriction alone does not guarantee quality.

questions.question: 11. In the MC Test item-generation workflow, why is explicit confirmation requested before final JSON output?
options: To confirm generation settings before final output
To let the system skip validation after generation
To remove the need for learning objectives
To require every learner to author a question set
To prevent instructors from changing topics later
questions.explanation: The finite-state workflow collects parameters step by step. Explicit confirmation ensures that topic, audience, item count, difficulty profile, option format, and context are clear before output is generated.

mini_glossary.Finite-state workflow: A controlled interaction sequence with defined phases.
mini_glossary.Configuration: The collection of parameters that determine the generated question set.
mini_glossary.Confirmation: An explicit check that the user accepts the collected parameters.

extended_explanation.title: Role of confirmation
extended_explanation.content: Confirmation reduces ambiguity in LLM-assisted generation. It is part of treating item generation as a controlled authoring workflow rather than a single free-form prompt.
steps: Collect generation parameters in a controlled order.
Check that the requested profile is coherent.
Ask for confirmation before producing the final structured output.
Generate after the parameters are stable.

questions.question: 12. A course team wants learners to receive targeted study advice instead of only a score. Which MC Test design principle best supports this goal?
options: To hide unanswered items completely on the learner dashboard
To disable all feedback until the course has fully ended
To report learner results only as one final percentage
To link results to topics, concepts, levels, and explanations
questions.explanation: MC Test is designed to move beyond a single total score. It links results to topics, concepts, cognitive levels, explanations, and study resources.

mini_glossary.Actionable feedback: Feedback that helps learners decide what to study next.
mini_glossary.Cognitive level: The type of thinking required by an item.
mini_glossary.Study planning: Choosing learning activities based on diagnosed strengths and weaknesses.

extended_explanation.title: From score reporting to guidance
extended_explanation.content: The formative value of MC Test lies in diagnostic detail. Learners can see not only whether they were wrong, but also what kind of knowledge or reasoning needs work.
steps: Recognize that a single score is too coarse for targeted learning.
Break performance down by topic and cognitive level.
Use explanations and glossaries to support remediation.
Turn patterns into concrete study targets.

questions.question: 13. Which situation best illustrates the purpose of the Pre-Answer Cooldown?
options: The learner must wait briefly before submitting an answer
The instructor cannot review any generated item drafts
The system deletes all old responses after the test ends
The dashboard hides incorrect answers from the learner
questions.explanation: The Pre-Answer Cooldown disables the Submit button for a short item-specific time. It is meant as reading support and a nudge against impulsive rapid guessing.

mini_glossary.Pre-Answer Cooldown: A short delay before submission becomes possible.
mini_glossary.Rapid guessing: Answering unusually quickly with reduced test-taking effort.
mini_glossary.Reading support: A design nudge that encourages attention to the item stem and options.

extended_explanation.title: Function of the pre-answer delay
extended_explanation.content: The Pre-Answer Cooldown does not prove that learners read deeply. It creates a small structural barrier against immediate, low-effort responses.
steps: Identify the timing point before answer submission.
Connect the delay to reading and reflection before choosing.
Distinguish it from post-answer feedback review.
Treat the mechanism as a hypothesis to be evaluated empirically.

questions.question: 14. What is the main purpose of the Time-Critical Override?
options: To block learners with accommodations from finishing attempts
To replace learner analytics with instructor-only reports
To disable cooldowns when time per remaining item is critical
To increase the scoring weight of unanswered items
To remove pacing mechanisms permanently from a course
questions.explanation: The Time-Critical Override relaxes cooldowns under strong time pressure. It prioritizes completion when fixed pacing would otherwise become a disadvantage.

mini_glossary.Time-Critical Override: A mechanism that disables cooldowns when time per remaining item is critically low.
mini_glossary.Adaptive pacing: Timing control that changes according to the learner's situation.
mini_glossary.Accommodation: An adjustment that supports fair access for learners with specific needs.

extended_explanation.title: Balancing pacing and completion
extended_explanation.content: Pacing mechanisms can support reflection, but they can also create barriers. The override is a safeguard against excessive rigidity in timed practice.
steps: Monitor remaining time and remaining items.
Detect when the available time per item becomes critically low.
Disable cooldowns to avoid preventing completion.
Keep pacing configurable for accessibility and course policy.

questions.question: 15. A concept has 10 associated items, and the learner answered all 10 with 7 correct. Under the default MC Test mastery rule described in the EDULEARN26 paper, how is the concept classified?
options: Classified as understood for the concept
Classified as not attempted for the concept
Classified as not understood for the concept
Excluded entirely from concept analytics
questions.explanation: The EDULEARN26 paper uses a 70% default heuristic: a concept counts as understood when at least 70% of its items are correct. Seven correct out of ten meets that threshold.

mini_glossary.Threshold: A cutoff value used for classification.
mini_glossary.Correctness rate: The proportion of items answered correctly for a concept.
mini_glossary.Heuristic: A practical rule of thumb rather than a fully validated mastery cut score.

extended_explanation.title: Applying the mastery rule
extended_explanation.content: The 70% rule in the paper is a configurable heuristic for study planning. It should not be interpreted as a psychometrically validated mastery cut score.
steps: Count the number of correct answers for the concept.
Divide correct answers by the relevant item total.
Compare the result with the 70% threshold.
Classify the concept as understood when the threshold is met.

questions.question: 16. Why does MC Test include a mini-glossary for individual items?
options: To store personally identifiable learner profile data
To rank learners publicly by terminology knowledge
To replace answer explanations with glossary entries
To provide vocabulary help at the point of feedback
questions.explanation: The mini-glossary gives learners short definitions of terms that matter for the item. It supports understanding directly at the point of feedback.

mini_glossary.Mini-glossary: A short set of item-specific term definitions.
mini_glossary.Vocabulary support: Help with understanding key terms used in a learning task.
mini_glossary.Feedback: Information given to learners about their response and reasoning.

extended_explanation.title: Role of item-level terminology support
extended_explanation.content: A mini-glossary is useful when misunderstanding a term could prevent learners from interpreting feedback. It supports formative learning without overloading the item stem.
steps: Identify terminology that may block understanding.
Provide concise definitions next to the item feedback.
Connect vocabulary support to the explanation.
Use cumulative resources for broader post-test review.

questions.question: 17. Which pair best describes the difference between topics and concepts in MC Test analytics?
options: Topics are learner names; concepts are test dates
Topics are response times; concepts are cooldown durations
Topics are broad units; concepts are fine-grained targets
Topics are incorrect options; concepts are correct options
Topics are privacy settings; concepts are database tables
questions.explanation: MC Test uses topics as broader curriculum units and concepts as finer-grained learning targets. That distinction supports both overview-level and remediation-level analytics.

mini_glossary.Topic: A broad content area in a curriculum.
mini_glossary.Concept: A specific learning target within a topic.
mini_glossary.Granularity: The level of detail at which data are represented.

extended_explanation.title: Understanding analytics granularity
extended_explanation.content: The topic-concept distinction helps learners move from general impressions to concrete remediation targets. It is central to the diagnostic funnel of MC Test.
steps: Use topics for broad performance patterns.
Use concepts for more specific diagnosis.
Combine both levels to guide study planning.
Avoid treating one concept as a complete topic.

questions.question: 18. An instructor wants to know whether two reviewers assign the same cognitive-level labels to items. Which measure is appropriate for this purpose?
options: Concept mastery percentage by topic area
Mean System Usability Scale composite score
Total database storage size in megabytes
Cohen's kappa for inter-rater label agreement
questions.explanation: Cohen's kappa is planned to assess agreement between double coders of cognitive-level labels. It helps show whether the labels are reliable enough to interpret.

mini_glossary.Double-coding: Independent classification of the same items by two reviewers.
mini_glossary.Cohen's kappa: A statistic for inter-rater agreement that accounts for chance agreement.
mini_glossary.Label reliability: The stability of assigned categories across reviewers.

extended_explanation.title: Evaluating label consistency
extended_explanation.content: Learner-facing cognitive analytics depend on stable labels. If labels are inconsistent, the resulting profiles may reflect coding noise rather than meaningful learning patterns.
steps: Have two instructors classify items with the same rubric.
Compare the assigned labels.
Calculate agreement statistics such as percent agreement and Cohen's kappa.
Treat low agreement as a warning before interpreting dashboards at scale.

questions.question: 19. What does a mean SUS score of 70.38 primarily indicate in the MC Test evaluation context?
options: Perfect reliability of cognitive-level item labels
Validated reduction of rapid-guessing item behavior
Preliminary evidence of acceptable perceived usability
Definitive proof of measurable learner learning gains
Complete elimination of all accessibility concerns
questions.explanation: The SUS result provides preliminary evidence about perceived usability. It does not establish learning gains, reduced rapid guessing, or label reliability.

mini_glossary.SUS: System Usability Scale, a questionnaire for perceived usability.
mini_glossary.Perceived usability: How usable participants judge a system to be.
mini_glossary.Learning gain: Improvement in knowledge or skill, usually measured with outcome data.

extended_explanation.title: Interpreting SUS cautiously
extended_explanation.content: Usability is necessary but not sufficient for educational effectiveness. MC Test therefore treats the SUS result as an initial signal, not as proof of learning impact.
steps: Identify SUS as a usability instrument.
Separate perceived usability from learning outcomes.
Recognize the pilot nature of the evidence.
Use follow-up studies for behavioral and learning claims.

questions.question: 20. Why is human instructor review still necessary after LLM-assisted item generation in MC Test?
options: Because validation alone cannot ensure educational quality
Because generated explanations cannot be learner-facing
Because JSON output makes instructor review impossible
Because local LLMs cannot produce any structured text
questions.explanation: Validation can check structure and some semantic constraints, but it cannot guarantee full educational quality. Human review remains necessary for correctness, alignment, and item quality.

mini_glossary.Human-in-the-loop: A process where human experts review or control automated outputs.
mini_glossary.Psychometric quality: Measurement quality of assessment items, including clarity and discrimination.
mini_glossary.Semantic validity: Whether an item meaningfully tests the intended concept.

extended_explanation.title: Limits of automation
extended_explanation.content: The MC Test workflow improves robustness but does not remove expert responsibility. Educational assessment still needs both technical validation and domain-informed judgment.
steps: Use the LLM to generate draft items.
Validate the generated structure automatically.
Repair formal errors when possible.
Let instructors review content, alignment, and educational quality.

questions.question: 21. A learner sees many unanswered items and a low percentage correct in one topic. Which interpretation is most appropriate?
options: The topic may need remediation, and unanswered items matter
The learner has mastered the topic because skipped items are neutral
The dashboard is useless because unanswered items are included
The learner clearly lacks all knowledge in that topic
questions.explanation: Unanswered items may indicate avoidance or time-management issues. The low score should be read together with answered and total counts.

mini_glossary.Unanswered item: An item for which the learner submitted no answer.
mini_glossary.Remediation: Targeted follow-up learning after identifying a weakness.
mini_glossary.Evidence count: The number of items supporting an interpretation.

extended_explanation.title: Reading topic performance carefully
extended_explanation.content: A topic-level bar is not just a score display. It helps learners see where they answered, where they skipped, and where follow-up study may be needed.
steps: Look at both correctness and unanswered counts.
Consider whether the pattern indicates content difficulty or avoidance.
Use evidence volume before drawing strong conclusions.
Plan remediation based on the pattern rather than the percentage alone.

questions.question: 22. Which design choice best supports pseudonymous participation in MC Test?
options: Publishing individual learner performance dashboards publicly
Sending generation prompts to external social platforms
Supporting progress tracking without personal accounts
Requiring public learner profiles for every session
Replacing session timestamps with learner full names
questions.explanation: Pseudonymous participation supports progress tracking while reducing the need for personal accounts. That fits privacy-aware use in institutional teaching contexts.

mini_glossary.Pseudonymous access: Use of an identifier that does not directly reveal a person's identity.
mini_glossary.Data minimization: Collecting and storing the data needed for the purpose.

extended_explanation.title: Privacy-aware access design
extended_explanation.content: Pseudonymity does not make data irrelevant, but it reduces direct identifiability. It is one part of a broader privacy-aware deployment strategy.
steps: Allow learners to participate without full personal accounts.
Track progress using pseudonymous identifiers.
Store minimal operational and assessment data.
Restrict access to authorized staff.

questions.question: 23. Why does MC Test use local MathJax rendering instead of an external content delivery network?
options: To prevent learners from seeing mathematical notation
To reduce leakage of usage data to external hosts
To make displayed formulas impossible for learners to copy
To replace the need for any database persistence layer
questions.explanation: Self-hosted MathJax helps avoid leaking usage data to external CDNs. That supports privacy-compliant rendering in institutional deployments.

mini_glossary.MathJax: A library for rendering mathematical notation in web pages.
mini_glossary.Content delivery network: External infrastructure used to serve web resources.
mini_glossary.Data leakage: Unintended exposure of user or usage data to another party.

extended_explanation.title: Rendering and privacy
extended_explanation.content: Privacy-aware architecture includes details beyond where the LLM runs. Even frontend rendering resources can create external data flows if they are loaded from third-party infrastructure.
steps: Identify that formula rendering can involve external resources.
Recognize that external requests may expose usage metadata.
Use self-hosted rendering to keep requests within institutional infrastructure.
Combine this with broader data minimization and access control.

questions.question: 24. In the on-premises architecture described in the EDULEARN26 paper, which database supports concurrent sessions and relational data?
options: A browser cookie without persistent server-side storage
PostgreSQL as the relational database persistence layer
A spreadsheet exported manually after each practice session
A slide deck embedded directly inside the application UI
A single static JSON file used for all session data
questions.explanation: The EDULEARN26 paper describes a Docker-based on-premises deployment that uses PostgreSQL for concurrent sessions and relational data such as user profiles, audit logs, and assessment records.

mini_glossary.On-premises deployment: Hosting within institutional infrastructure rather than on external cloud APIs.
mini_glossary.Concurrent session: A situation where multiple users interact with the system at the same time.

extended_explanation.title: Why relational persistence matters
extended_explanation.content: The paper's institutional deployment needs structured storage for sessions, analytics, logging, and future evaluation. PostgreSQL is the persistence layer named for that on-premises architecture.
steps: Recognize that multi-user practice requires reliable storage.
Link the paper's Docker-based deployment to PostgreSQL.
Distinguish this architecture from lightweight file-based storage.
Connect persistence to sessions, audit logs, and assessment records.

questions.question: 25. A course team wants to evaluate whether cooldowns reduce rapid guessing. Which data source is most relevant?
options: The metadata title of the question set only
The total number of mini-glossary terms only
The learner's final percentage score summary only
System logs of response times and learner behavior
questions.explanation: Rapid guessing is inferred from response-time and behavior patterns. System logs are therefore more relevant than final scores alone.

mini_glossary.Response time: The time a learner takes to answer an item.
mini_glossary.System log: A recorded trace of user actions and system events.
mini_glossary.Rapid-guessing indicator: A signal suggesting unusually low response effort.

extended_explanation.title: Choosing evidence for behavioral evaluation
extended_explanation.content: Cooldowns are design hypotheses. Evaluating them requires behavioral data that show how learners interact with items under different pacing conditions.
steps: Define rapid guessing as a time-related behavior.
Collect response-time and navigation data.
Compare behavior with and without pacing mechanisms.
Interpret results as behavioral evidence rather than direct proof of learning.

questions.question: 26. A learner performs well in most topics but weakly on analysis across nearly all topics. What is the most defensible interpretation?
options: The dashboard proves the analysis items are psychometrically invalid
The learner should ignore all cognitive-level dashboard information
The learner likely has a broad analysis gap rather than one topic gap
The learner has no problem because overall topic scores remain strong
questions.explanation: Weak analysis scores across topics suggest a pattern tied to cognitive demand, not just one topic. Still check evidence volume and item quality before drawing strong conclusions.
questions.concept: Distinguishing content gaps from process gaps

mini_glossary.Process-level difficulty: A weakness related to a type of thinking rather than one content area.
mini_glossary.Content gap: A weakness in a specific topic or domain area.
mini_glossary.Evidence volume: The amount of item data supporting a dashboard interpretation.
mini_glossary.Analysis item: An item that requires interpretation, comparison, or inference.

extended_explanation.title: Reasoning across dashboard dimensions
extended_explanation.content: The key analytical move is to separate what the learner is studying from how the learner is thinking. Repeated weakness in analysis across topics is more consistent with a cognitive-level difficulty than with a single content gap.
steps: Compare performance across topics.
Compare performance across cognitive levels.
Look for a pattern that repeats across multiple topics.
Check whether enough items support the pattern.
Consider item quality before making strong claims.

questions.question: 27. Which combination best represents the MC Test quality-assurance logic for LLM-generated items?
options: Random generation, no metadata, and post-hoc score aggregation
Free-form prompting, immediate publication, and learner ranking
External API dependence, hidden prompts, and no repair strategy
Schema-constrained generation, validation, harmonization, and review
Single-score feedback, no explanations, and no cognitive labels
questions.explanation: MC Test combines technical constraints with human review. The pipeline aims to produce parseable items, fix structural problems, reduce option cues, and preserve instructor control.

mini_glossary.Quality assurance: Processes used to detect and reduce errors or weaknesses.
mini_glossary.Schema-constrained generation: Generation constrained by a predefined output structure.
mini_glossary.Option harmonization: Revision of answer options to reduce unintended cues.

extended_explanation.title: Analyzing the QA chain
extended_explanation.content: The logic is layered. No single mechanism guarantees high-quality items, so MC Test combines automated checks, targeted revisions, and human expertise.
steps: Start with constrained generation rather than free-form text.
Validate generated JSON for structure and answer-index correctness.
Repair structural or semantic drift where possible.
Harmonize options to reduce cueing.
Require instructor review before release.

questions.question: 28. A local LLM backend produces syntactically valid JSON but sometimes changes the intended difficulty distribution. Which response best fits the MC Test design philosophy?
options: Remove cognitive levels from the item model to simplify generation
Accept the output because valid JSON is sufficient
Add semantic validation for the requested difficulty profile
Reject local models as unusable for education
questions.explanation: Valid syntax alone is not enough. MC Test also needs semantic checks, such as whether generated weights match the requested difficulty profile.

mini_glossary.Semantic validation: Checking whether content satisfies intended meaning and constraints.
mini_glossary.Difficulty profile: The requested distribution of item weights or difficulty levels.
mini_glossary.Local model calibration: Adapting prompts and checks to the behavior of a local LLM.

extended_explanation.title: Going beyond parseability
extended_explanation.content: The design philosophy treats structured output as necessary but insufficient. Educational validity also depends on whether the generated item set matches the intended didactic specification.
steps: Recognize that parseable JSON can still violate educational constraints.
Identify the mismatch between requested and generated weights.
Add semantic validation for the difficulty profile.
Use repair or regeneration when the profile is not satisfied.

questions.question: 29. A learner answers quickly and incorrectly, then wants to move on without reading the explanation. Which combination of MC Test mechanisms most directly addresses this behavior?
options: Topic labels paired with public source-code release
PostgreSQL storage paired with Docker Compose deployment
Post-Answer Cooldown paired with extended feedback review
Pseudonymous login paired with locally hosted MathJax
SUS scoring paired with MIT open-source licensing
questions.explanation: The Post-Answer Cooldown creates a short review window after submission. Extended feedback can support reflection by explaining the reasoning behind the item.

mini_glossary.Post-Answer Cooldown: A delay after submitting an answer before moving on.
mini_glossary.Extended feedback: More detailed explanation, often with step-by-step reasoning.
mini_glossary.Reflection window: A short period intended for reviewing feedback before continuing.
mini_glossary.Impulsive responding: Answering and moving on with little engagement.

extended_explanation.title: Matching behavior to intervention
extended_explanation.content: The issue is not only the initial response speed but also the lack of feedback engagement afterward. Post-answer pacing and meaningful explanations address the reflection phase of the learning cycle.
steps: Identify the behavior as skipping feedback after a fast incorrect answer.
Select a mechanism that operates after submission.
Use the delay to create time for feedback review.
Pair the delay with explanations that make the review useful.

questions.question: 30. Which statement best captures the current evidence status of MC Test?
options: It has proven large learning gains across STEM disciplines
It offers design contribution with preliminary usability evidence
It has eliminated the need for psychometric item analysis
It is validated as a high-stakes examination platform
questions.explanation: MC Test currently offers a system design contribution and preliminary perceived-usability evidence. Claims about learning outcomes, reduced rapid guessing, and cognitive-label reliability still need follow-up studies.

mini_glossary.Design-oriented contribution: A contribution focused on system model, architecture, and implementation logic.
mini_glossary.Preliminary evidence: Early evidence that is useful but not yet conclusive.
mini_glossary.Behavioral validation: Empirical testing of how users actually behave in the system.
mini_glossary.Psychometric analysis: Statistical evaluation of item quality and measurement properties.

extended_explanation.title: Assessing claim strength
extended_explanation.content: A rigorous reading separates what has been implemented from what has been empirically established. MC Test is promising, but its behavioral and learning effects still need systematic validation.
steps: Separate system design from outcome validation.
Treat usability evidence as preliminary.
Identify rapid guessing and learning gains as planned evaluation targets.
Avoid overstating what the current evidence supports.
